coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of graphene quantum dots/silicon nanowires nanohybrids for photoelectrochemical detection of microcystin-LR
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Junping
Zhao, Huimin
Quan, Xie
Zhang, Yaobin
Yu, Hongtao
Chen, Shuo
coredata.dc:description: A photoelectrochemical immunosensor was developed by incorporating graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and highly oriented silicon nanowires (SiNWs) for the determination of microcystin-LR (MC-LR) in water samples. The GQDs/SiNWs heterostructure was employed for signal transduction and biocompatible nanoscaffold for antibody (Ab) immobilization. The results showed that the fabricated GQDs/SiNWs electrode exhibited better photoelectrochemical performance than that of SiNWs. The specific recognition of MC-LR affected the optoelectronic properties of the Ab/GQDs/SiNWs electrode, leading to the photocurrent decreases. In the linear range from 0.1μgL−1 to 10μgL−1, the photocurrent decrease was proportional to the concentration of microcystin-LR and the linear regression equation was Y =59.67−6.08X with a limit of detection of 0.055μgL−1. The immunosensor possessed good sensitivity, selectivity and acceptable reusability for the determination of microcystin-LR, which was successfully applied in determining MC-LR in real water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoelectrochemical immunoassay
Silicon naonowire
Graphene quantum dot
Microcystin-LR

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ab immobilization onto the GQDs/SiNWs electrode
Analysis of microcystin-LR in spiked water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21277016 ) and the Research Project of Chinese Ministry of Education (No. 113017A ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Development of the GQDs/SiNW nanohybrids-based photoelectrochemical immunoassay for microcystin-LR detection.
SEM of top view (A) and cross-sectional images (B) of SiNWs arrays.
TEM image of SiNW array (A) and a single SiNW (B).
(A) TEM image of GQDs and the inset was the line profile from the area marked with the rectangular frame. (B) The UV–vis absorption spectrum (a) and the fluorescence emission spectrum (b) of GQDs.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms of GQDs/SiNWs nanohybrids under simulated solar light and visible light. (B) Linear sweep voltammetry of SiNWs and GQDs/SiNWs nanohybrids. All measurements were performed in 0.1M PBS containing 0.1M AA.

Nyquist plots for GQDs/SiNWs electrode (a) and GQDs/SiNWs/Ab (b). EIS measurements were performed in the frequency range from 20mHz to 10kHz at a signal amplitude of 5mV. All measurements were performed in 0.1M PBS containing 0.1M AA.
The Effect of incubation time on the photoelectrochemical responses for 1.0μgL−1 microcystin-LR.

Photoelectrochemical measurements for microcystin-LR in the range of 0.1–10μgL−1 from the top to bottom (A) and the calibration curve of photoelectrochemical detection of microcystin-LR (B). Each data point represents the average value obtained from three measurements, and error bars represent the standard deviation. All photocurrent measurements were performed in 0.1M PBS containing 0.1M AA and the working potential was 1.0V.

Selectivity of the photoelectrochemical immunoassay of MC-LR, MC-RR, MC-YR, and nodularin at concentration of 1μgL−1, respectively. Error bars showed standard deviation of three replicate determinations. All photoelectrochemical measurements were performed in 0.10M PBS containing 0.1M AA and the working potential was 1.0V.
Detection of microcystin-LR in environmental water samples from Dalian reservoir. SD, standard deviation (n =3 replicates).

head.ce:title: Fabrication of graphene quantum dots/silicon nanowires nanohybrids for photoelectrochemical detection of microcystin-LR

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering (Ministry of Education, China), School of Environmental Science and Technology, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering (Ministry of Education, China), School of Environmental Science and Technology, Dalian University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A photoelectrochemical immunosensor was developed by incorporating graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and highly oriented silicon nanowires (SiNWs) for the determination of microcystin-LR (MC-LR) in water samples. The GQDs/SiNWs heterostructure was employed for signal transduction and biocompatible nanoscaffold for antibody (Ab) immobilization. The results showed that the fabricated GQDs/SiNWs electrode exhibited better photoelectrochemical performance than that of SiNWs. The specific recognition of MC-LR affected the optoelectronic properties of the Ab/GQDs/SiNWs electrode, leading to the photocurrent decreases. In the linear range from 0.1μgL−1 to 10μgL−1, the photocurrent decrease was proportional to the concentration of microcystin-LR and the linear regression equation was Y =59.67−6.08X with a limit of detection of 0.055μgL−1. The immunosensor possessed good sensitivity, selectivity and acceptable reusability for the determination of microcystin-LR, which was successfully applied in determining MC-LR in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: One-dimensional semiconductor nanowires (NWs) have attracted tremendous attention as building blocks to enable extraordinary progress for the fabrication of electrochemical biosensor [1,2], field-effect transistor biosensor [3] and photoluminescence biosensor [4]. Among various NWs-based biosensors reported so far, notable achievements have been made using single crystalline silicon nanowires (SiNWs) based on their intrinsic advantages of SiNWs, including low toxicity, high surface area (vast surface-to-bulk ratio), ease of surface modification and biocompatibility, make them extremely attractive to maintain the activity of biomolecules for biosensor applications [5,6]. Due to their robust electronic and optical properties, semiconductive SiNWs can be used for highly selective and ultrasensitive biosensor construction, which were capable of detecting specific targets by acting as transducers of biomolecular binding events. For example, vertically-aligned SiNWs field effect transistors use electric field changes as sensing signals without requiring additional molecules to generate detection signals [7]. On the basis of this principle, significant achievements have been obtained for the label-free electrical detection of biomolecular interactions [3,8–10]. Besides, as biocompatible sensing matrix and nanoscaffold, SiNWs have been employed for the fabrication of electrochemical biosensors by determining the catalytic enzyme activity [11,12]. In these detection systems, the amounts of target were directly related to the current density.

Despite these interesting characteristics mentioned above, several drawbacks limit the applications of SiNWs-based biosensors in aqueous solution. In fact, the rapid formation of an insulating oxide layer on the Si surface in aqueous condition causes the inherent instability of pristine Si substrate and decreases the devices conductivity [13]. To prepare SiNWs-based biosensors with long-term stability and high efficiency, one of the major challenges is to stabilize the structure via appropriate surface modification to slow further oxidation and improve analytical performance during biosensing process. Thus, coating a transparent charge substrate on the surface of SiNWs to serve as a protective layer is a feasible method to overcome corrosion of Si. Moreover, the combination of SiNWs with protective materials used for charge transfer could provide functional hetero-junctions for potential applications in biosensors. Thereby as an effective approach, the electronic and optical properties of SiNWs based nanohybrids semiconductors have been investigated intensively, some of which have been applied for the construction of photodevices [14–16] and biosensors [17–20]. For instance, the fabrication of biosensor using SiNWs nanohybrids with metal nanoparticles [17,18] and semiconductor nanomaterials can efficiently restrain Si surface oxidation to improve the electronic/optical performance of SiNWs. In particular, due to the good chemical stability and size tunable optical properties, the nanohybrid with attractive merits made of CdTe quantum dots (QDs) decorated with SiNWs exhibits superior photostability [19,20]. Thus the unique advantages to form nanohybrid heterostructures with QDs may offer a promising way to develop QDs/SiNWs as novel building blocks for biosensor applications.

Compared to conventional semiconductor quantum dots, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with distinct advantages of biocompatibility, low toxicity, photostability and ease of bioconjugation have been used as effective biosensing platform for fluorescent and electrochemical biosensors [21,22]. Recently, a luminescence resonance energy transfer (LRET) immunosensor has been developed based on the interaction between GQDs and graphene, which was used for sensitive and selective detection of IgG [23]. Besides, GQDs with large solubility, tunable band gap, have been used as photovoltaic material [24] or combined with other semiconductor materials by forming nanohybrids to fabricate high performance photovoltaic devices [25]. As a proof-of-concept example, the incorporation of poly(3-hexylthiophene) with GQDs provided an effective interface for charge separation and a pathway for electron transport, which have been confirmed by the significantly increased photocurrent as compared to the device fabricated with pure poly(3-hexylthiophene) [26]. These studies indicated that the improved photoelectrochemical response could be attributed to the fact that the charge separation was enhanced at the interface of these novel architectures due to the build-in electric field. These unique properties make GQDs as a promising candidate to synthesize novel GQDs/SiNWs semiconductor nanohybrid, of which GQDs with the intrinsic properties of both carbon quantum dots and graphene could facilitate charge separation and act as sufficient barriers to prevent silicon oxidation. Moreover, the oxygen containing groups on the surface of the GQDs impart them with excellent water solubility and facile functionalizations, which has potential advantages with respect to the applications of fluorescent and electrochemical biosensors [21–23]. Based on these investigations, the formation of GQDs/SiNWs nanohybrid may acquire attractive semiconductor properties, which can be further employed as a novel matrix for the fabrication of SiNWs-based biosensors for diverse applications with high analytical performance and stability in aqueous solution.

In this study, a photoelectrochemical immunosensor was developed on the basis of GQDs/SiNWs nanohybrids to generate photocurrents under light simulation. The device utilized antibody as capture probe immobilized on the GQDs/SiNWs electrode surface to selectively recognize target. Microcystin-LR (MC-LR), a toxic heptapeptide, was selected as the target molecule, which has caused serious health problems around the world [27]. Since the traditional techniques for MC-LR detection usually need tedious procedures for sample pretreatment or preconcentration, thus, it is worthwhile to explore a sensitive and selective method for MC-LR determination. This proposed photoelectrochemical technique provided an efficient approach for quantitative determination of MC-LR according to the photocurrent changes. Under light irradiation, the photoactivated excitons were created by the excited GQDs/SiNWs nanohybrids, and a photocurrent was generated correspondingly due to the charge separation. Furthermore, photo-generated holes can be steered to react with electron donor to facilitate electron/hole separation and electrons transfer, leading to the enhancement of anodic photocurrent under positive bias. After the addition of MC-LR in the detection system, the nonelectroactive MC-LR absorbed on the fabricated electrode caused steric hindrance and restrain the electron transfer, thus resulting in a photocurrent decrease. The amounts of MC-LR could be measured directly based on the decrease of photocurrent intensities. We demonstrated that the sensing platform, entailing GQDs/SiNWs nanohybrids, can be used as an immunoassay tool with the potential to serve as a cost-effective, rapid, and sensitive system for photoelectrochemical determination of water toxins.

n-Type silicon wafer (phosphorus-doped, Si(111) orientation, 0.1–0.5Ωcm resistivity) was purchased from China Electronics Science & Technology Group No. 46 Institute (Tianjin, China). Commercial fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass (2.2mm thickness, 15Ω/sq sheet resistance) was obtained from Geao Co. (Wuhan, China). MC-LR, MC-RR, MC-YR, nodularin (all minimum 95% by HPLC) and monoclonal antibody (Clone MC8C10) were purchased from Express Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was obtained from Beijing Wanyumeilan Scientific Inc. (Beijing, China). Glutaraldehyde (25wt% aqueous solution) was obtained from J&K Chemical Ltd. Pristine graphite powder was obtained from Shenzhen Nanotech Port Co., Ltd. (Shenzhen, China). Chemicals with analytic reagent grade such as ascorbic acid (AA), NH4F, AgNO3, HCl, HNO3, HF, H2O2, NaH2PO4·2H2O, Na2HPO4·12H2O were purchased from Bodi Chemical Holding Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). 0.1M phosphate buffered saline (PBS) solution was prepared by mixing nitrogen-saturated stock solutions of 0.1M NaH2PO4 and 0.1M Na2HPO4 in different proportions. All solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (resistivity>18.2MΩcm−1, Laikie Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). Real water samples were collected from a reservoir near the city of Dalian and stored in darkness at 4°C prior to analysis.

The morphologies of samples were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI-Tecnai G2 F30S-Twin). The UV–vis spectra were performed by a Shimadzu UV-2450 UV/Vis spectrophotometer with an integrating sphere. The fluorescence spectra were performed by a Hitachi UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Japan). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and photoelectrochemical (PEC) measurements were performed in a three-electrode system that was connected to a CHI 650B electrochemical station (CH Instruments, Shanghai Chenhua, China). The fabricated electrode served as the working electrode, a platinum foil as a counter electrode, and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as a reference electrode. EIS was performed under an oscillation potential of 5mV over the frequency range of 10kHz to 0.01Hz. The amplitude of the alternate voltage was 5mV. For PEC measurements, a high-pressure Xe short-arc lamp (CHF-XM35-150W, Beijing Changtuo Co., China) was used as the light source to simulate solar light. The light intensity was 100mWcm−2. The transient photocurrent responses were recorded for several on–off cycles of illumination. LC/MS/MS was used to analyze the MC-LR in real water samples by Agilent G6410 Triple Quadrupole Mass Spectrometer with an electrospray ionization source, which consisted of an HPLC Agilent 1200 series. The unique identifier ion (m/z =995.2) was chosen for selected ion monitoring (SIM). All measurements were performed at room temperature.

Silicon wafer (1cm×1cm) was cleaned by acetone, absolute ethylalcohol and ultrapure water, respectively. The SiNWs were fabricated by chemical etching in 5M NH4F and 0.02M AgNO3 mixture. After reacted for 60min at 50°C in thermostatic water bath, the products were dipped into the mixture of HNO3 and H2O (1:1 in volume) to remove excess Ag. After that, the samples were immersed into 5% HF solution for 1min, rinsed with water and finally dried in a N2 [14]. GQDs were prepared according to previous method and the obtained light yellow-green supernatant of GQDs was further functionalized with carboxyl groups [23]. In short, 0.05g NaOH and 0.1g ClCH2COOH were added into 20mL GQDs solution, followed by bath sonication for 3h to covert hydroxyl groups into carboxyl groups, and the resultant product was neutralized with HCl. For the preparation of GQDs/SiNWs nanohybrids, spin-coating of a suspension of GQDs on SiNWs was performed to form a thin layer of GQDs on SiNWs surface. After dried with nitrogen gas, an annealing process was performed at 300°C for 15min to enhance the adhesion of GQDs on SiNWs. The control experiment used FTO as the substrate for electrode buildup, after spreading aqueous GQDs over FTO, the as-prepared GQDs/FTO electrode was dried with nitrogen gas.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ab immobilization onto the GQDs/SiNWs electrode
ce:section.ce:para: For high density of antibody immobilization, 20μL glutaraldehyde (2.5wt%) in PBS was mixed with 20μL 0.1mgmL−1 concentration of MC-LR antibody for 30min, and then the mixture was dropped on the electrode surface [28]. After incubated for 12h at room temperature, Ab/GQDs/SiNWs electrode were rinsed with PBS to remove unbind Ab. Subsequently, a blocking process was done using 2mgmL−1 concentration of BSA (wt% in PBS). Finally the electrode was dried under nitrogen gas.

The fabrication of Ab/SiNWs was prepared as a control experiment without GQDs modification, and the procedure was performed as described above. The as-prepared SiNWs was incubated with the mixture of 20μL glutaraldehyde (2.5wt%) and 20μL 0.1mgmL−1 antibody for 2h at room temperature. After blocked by BSA solution, the electrode was dried under nitrogen gas.

To investigate the sensitivity of the immunoassay method, the photoelectrochemical immunoassay of microcystin-LR was performed using the fabricated Ab/GQDs/SiNWs electrode. Briefly, 25μL of microcystin-LR in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0) with various concentrations (0.01μgL−1, 0.05μgL−1, 1.0μgL−1, 3.0μgL−1, 5.0μgL−1, 8.0μgL−1, and 10.0μgL−1) was dropped onto the Ab/GQDs/SiNWs electrode to form the immunocomplex. After incubated at 37°C for 60min, the electrode was rinsed with 0.1M PBS containing 0.05% Tween-20 to minimize nonspecific adsorption. Photoelectrochemical measurements were performed in a quartz reactor with a volume of 20mL. 0.1M PBS containing 0.1M AA was employed as the supporting electrolyte. All the photoelectrochemical measurements were performed at room temperature.

Scheme 1 illustrated the synthetic steps to develop the photoelectrochemical immunoassay for microcystin-LR detection. SiNWs arrays were firstly prepared on the Si wafer surface followed by the linkage of GQDs. The resulting GQDs/SiNWs nanohybrids served as bioaffinitive sensing interface and nanoscaffold for the immobilization of Ab. In the present of microcystin-LR, the immunosensor system can specifically recognize the target microcystin-LR. The unique formation of immunocomplex on the sensing interface can increase the electron transfer resistance of fabricated electrode, leading to a distinct decrease in the photoelectrochemical current density. This signal change was finally recorded and used to quantify the amounts of microcystin-LR. As a result, the proposed system showed sensitive and specific microcystin-LR detection.

The SEM images in Fig. 1 showed the top view and cross-sectional images of SiNWs arrays, indicating that the SiNWs arrays are produced vertically on the Si wafer with high density. As shown in Fig. 1A, the morphologies of the as-prepared SiNWs showed a large-scale of oriented SiNWs arrays. The morphologies of vertically aligned SiNWs arrays were shown in Fig. 1B. The average length of the SiNWs was 9.7μm, and their average diameter was 80nm. Fig. 2 showed the TEM images of individual SiNWs bundles. It was found that the TEM micrographs of SiNWs had bamboo-like structures with an average diameter of about 80nm, which further demonstrated the successful preparation of typical SiNWs. The morphologies of GQDs were shown in Fig. 3 . High-resolution TEM examination in Fig. 3 demonstrated that free-standing GQDs were highly crystallized. The well-crystalline GQDs had a less than 0.32nm lattice spacing, which was smaller than the interlayer spacing of bulk graphite (0.334nm) [29], suggesting that GQDs were actually individual graphene.

The results of photoelectrochemical measurements showed that GQDs/SiNWs nanohybrids obviously improved the photoelectrochemical performance compared to SiNWs. Fig. 4A showed the cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements of GQDs/SiNWs electrode under simulated solar light and visible light irradiation. According to the CV curves, both solar light and visible light can generate photocurrent, but the photocurrent obtained by solar light was 0.94 times higher than that of visible light at potential of 1.0V. Therefore, solar light was selected as the light source in the following experiments. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was measured to evaluate both of photoelectrochemical behaviors under shuttering and unshuttering the light source, which was used as an effective approach to compare the photo-response of GQDs/SiNWs and SiNWs. As shown in Fig. 4B, under illumination, anodic photocurrents were observed at the positive bias potential, and the rise/fall of anodic photocurrents corresponded well to illumination being switched on and off. Moreover, a significant increase in the photocurrent under light illumination was observed through-out the potential window. The anodic photocurrent obtained by the GQDs/SiNWs electrode was about twice of SiNWs at potential of 1.0V. It has been reported that GQDs with controllable size showed broad light response [33], thus pure GQDs was selected as a control to investigate their photoelectrochemical performance. The results in Fig. S1 showed that pure GQDs was indeed able to generate photocurrent under light irradiation, however, the transient photocurrent response of GQDs/FTO was five times lower than that of GQDs/SiNWs material. Since the photocurrent was mostly limited by charge generation and transportation, thereby, the photocurrent enhancement by GQDs/SiNWs could be attributed to the advantage of GQDs such as light-absorbing and hole-transporting [25]. In addition, the similar behaviors were obtained by the integration of SiNWs with TiO2 [30,31] and graphene [32], which demonstrated that these nanohybrid heterostructures significantly improved the charge separation and minimized recombination, leading to the enhanced photocurrent and the stability of SiNWs in aqueous solution. As a result, the fabrication of GQDs/SiNWs is a general and efficient approach to control Si passivation and facilitate charge generation, thereby enhancing the photoelectrochemical performance. On the basis of these investigations, GQDs/SiNWs can be potentially harnessed as a novel nanohybrid for the fabrication of photoelectrochemical biosensor. Besides, electron donor can affect the photo-response of the fabricated electrode. The results in Fig. S2 showed that the photocurrent obtained by the GQDs/SiNWs electrode in PBS with electron donors (AA molecules) was obviously higher than that in PBS only. These results indicated that AA can be rapidly oxidized by photogenerated holes at the electrode surface under illumination, and electron transfer rate became fast with the applied potential. Finally, the photogenerated electrons were collected to give an initial surge of the photocurrent.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was a powerful technique for the investigation of the interface properties of electrodes. The electron transfer resistance (Rct) was estimated from the diameter of the semicircle, indicating that the charge transfer occurred across the interface. The Nyquist plots of GQDs/SiNWs and Ab/GQDs/SiNWs were shown in Fig. 5 , which showed that the conductance of working electrode decreased after the electrode was modified by Ab. Compared with GQDs/SiNWs electrode, the diameter of semicircle in impedance spectrum drastically increased according to the modification of Ab, which might attributed to the hindrance of the macromolecular structure of Ab to electron transfer. The Rct increase also confirmed the successful immobilization of Ab on electrode.

Given that the incubation time was a critical factor for successful immunoassay, the incubation time for the immunoreaction was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 6 , at 1.0μgL−1 of microcystin-LR, the photocurrent decreased gradually as the incubation time increased, and then reached a plateau at 60min. No obvious photocurrent change was observed with longer period of incubation time. As a result, 60min was selected for the immunoreaction.

The fabricated photoelectrochemical system displayed high photoelectrochemical responses to microcystin-LR. Since the target microcystin-LR molecules are nonelectroactive species, the formation of immunocomplex by microcystin-LR and antibodies would create a barrier for the electrochemical process, leading to a photocurrent decrease (Fig. S3). With the increasing concentration of microcystin-LR in the present system, interfacial electron transfer would be further hindered, resulting in gradual photocurrent decrease. Hence, the photoelectrochemical immunoassay can be accomplished by tracking the transduction photocurrents that were directly correlated with the concentrations of microcystin-LR. To investigate the sensitivity of the presented photoelectrochemical immunoasay, the linear range of the immunoasay was obtained prone to the saturation of fabricated electrode. Fig. 7 illustrated the relationship of the photocurrents on the standard MC-LR concentration, showing a quantitative assay for MC-LR. According to the formation of immunocomplex, photocurrents decreased proportionally to the concentration of microcystin-LR in logarithmic scale with the linear range from 0.1μgL−1 to 10μgL−1. The linear regression equation for MC-LR determination was Y =59.67−6.08X with a reasonably good correlation (R 2 =0.96). The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.055μgL−1 (defined as 3σ/slope, with σ being the standard deviation of the blank samples), which had met the standard of the world health organization (WHO) requirement for MC-LR content in drinking water (1μgL−1). Taking together, the above results demonstrated that the developed photoelectrochemical immunoassay can quantitatively detect microcystin-LR with satisfied sensitivity.

The photoelectrochemical immunoassay showed good selectivity for microcystin-LR determination. The photocurrents of the GQDs/SiNWs/Ab electrode to MC-RR, MC-YR, or nodularin were measured as interfering agents (Fig. 8 ). Compared to photocurrent of MC-LR, the photocurrents of MC-RR, MC-YR and nodularin were 25.3%, 28.2% and 10.7% of MC-LR, showing acceptable specificity. In order to examine the reusability of the working electrode, 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0) was used to wash the GQDs/SiNWs/Ab/MC-LR electrode surface extensively, and then the electrode was immersed into aqueous solution of glycine–HCl buffer (0.1M, pH 2.8) with constant stirring to dissociate the antigen–antibody complex. Finally, the as-renewed electrode could maintain 90.5% of the initial photocurrent value after three immunoassay measurements, exhibiting accepted regeneration. These results indicated that GQDs was a beneficial element to oxidation resistance for SiNWs and the GQDs/SiNWs nanohybrid can serve as a biocompatible sensing matrix and nanoscaffold, leading to satisfactory analytical performance and high stability in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of microcystin-LR in spiked water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The determination of microcystin-LR in real water samples was performed using the developed photoelectrochemical immunoassay method and LC/MS/MS instrument, respectively. As shown in Table 1 , the results obtained by photoelectrochemical immunoassay for the quantification of MC-LR (0.489±0.05μgL−1) were well consistent with the values obtained by LC/MS/MS (0.494±0.0176μgL−1), demonstrating the reliability and potential applicability of the photoelectrochemical immunoassay for the quantification of MC-LR. Moreover, the average recoveries obtained by photoelectrochemical immunoassay and LC/MS/MS measurements were in the range of 97.8–117.2% and 99.6–116.2%, respectively. Compared to LC/MS/MS measurements, this immunoassay would be reliable for determining MC-LR in environmental water samples.

In summary, GQDs/SiNWs nanohybrids proved to be excellent materials with high analytical performance for microcystin-LR detection. The photocurrents were found to decrease linearly in the concentrations range from 0.01μgL−1 to 10μgL−1 with the limit of detection of 0.055μgL−1 for microcystin-LR. This photoelectrochemical immunosensor exhibited high sensitivity, selectivity and stability. This strategy could be a promising candidate for probing other photoelectrochemical immunoassay for chemical analysis, clinical diagnosis, food safety industry and environmental monitoring.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21277016) and the Research Project of Chinese Ministry of Education (No. 113017A).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2014.02.046.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Vapor–liquid–solid grown silica nanowire based electrochemical glucose biosensor
An excellent enzyme biosensor based on Sb-doped SnO2 nanowires
Double-gate nanowire field effect transistor for a biosensor
Enhanced glucose detection using enzyme-immobilized ZnO/ZnS core/sheath nanowires
Optical properties of individual silicon nanowires for photonic devices
IGZO nanoparticle-modified silicon nanowires as extended-gate field-effect transistor pH sensors
DNA sensing by silicon nanowire: charge layer distance dependence
Silicon nanowire arrays for label-free detection of DNA
Silicon nanowire biosensor for highly sensitive and rapid detection of Dengue virus
Voltammetric detection of cancer biomarkers exemplified by interleukin-10 and osteopontin with silica nanowires
Silicon nanowires for high-sensitivity glucose detection
Construction and characterization of porous SiO2/hydrogel hybrids as optical biosensors for rapid detection of bacteria
A structured macroporous silicon/graphene heterojunction for efficient photoconversion
Platinum nanoparticle decorated silicon nanowires for efficient solar energy conversion
Enhanced photoelectrochemical hydrogen production from silicon nanowire array photocathode
A novel biosensor based on gold nanoparticles modified silicon nanowire arrays
A silicon nanowire-based electrochemical glucose biosensor with high electrocatalytic activity and sensitivity
ZnO/silicon nanowire hybrids extended-gate field-effect transistors as pH sensors
Highly luminescent water-dispersible silicon nanowires for long-term immunofluorescent cellular imaging
Graphene quantum dots as a new substrate for immobilization and direct electrochemistry of glucose oxidase: application to sensitive glucose determination
Electrochemiluminescence of blue-luminescent graphene quantum dots and its application in ultrasensitive aptasensor for adenosine triphosphate detection
A universal immunosensing strategy based on regulation of the interaction between graphene and graphene quantum dots
Large, solution-processable graphene quantum dots as light absorbers for photovoltaics
ZnO/graphene quantum dot solid-state solar cell
An electrochemical avenue to green-luminescent graphene quantum dots as potential electron-acceptors for photovoltaics
Comparative toxicity evaluation of cyanobacterial cyclic peptide toxin microcystin variants (LR, RR, YR) in mice
Photoelectrochemical immunoassay for microcystin-LR based on a fluorine-doped tin oxide glass electrode modified with a CdS-graphene composite
Facile synthesis of water-soluble, highly fluorescent graphene quantum dots as a robust biological label for stem cells
Effective utilization of visible light (including λ >600nm) in phenol degradation with p-silicon nanowire/TiO2 core/shell heterojunction array cathode
High density n-Si/n-TiO2 core/shell nanowire arrays with enhanced photoactivity
Graphene/silicon nanowire Schottky junction for enhanced light harvesting
Graphene-based materials for hydrogen generation from light-driven water splitting

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Junping Tian is a PhD candidate in environmental engineering at School of Environmental Science and Technology, Dalian University of Technology, China. Her current research interest is functional nanomaterials-based biosensor.

Huimin Zhao received the PhD in environmental engineering from Dalian University of Technology, China, in 2002. She is currently a professor at School of Environmental Science and Technology. Her research interests are functional material and their application in environmental monitor.

Xie Quan received his BSc in chemistry from Northeastern Normal University in 1982 and MSc in environmental science in 1986 from Nanjing University, China. He completed his PhD in 2000 at University of Graz, Austria. He received “Grantee of the National Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars” in 2005 and professor of “Chang Jiang Scholars Program” in 2006. His interests focus on environmental material, water pollution control, and environmental monitoring technology.

Yaobin Zhang received the PhD in environmental engineering from Dalian University of Technology, China, in 2005. He is currently a professor at School of Environmental Science and Technology. His research interest is advanced technologies for water pollution control.

Hongtao Yu received his PhD in environmental engineering from Dalian University of Technology, China, in 2009. He is currently an associate professor at School of Environmental Science and Technology. His research interests include photocatalysis, environmental applications of nanomaterials.

Shuo Chen received the PhD in environmental engineering from Dalian University of Technology, China, in 2002. She is currently an associate professor at School of Environmental Science and Technology. Her research interests are nanomaterial and environmental monitoring technology.
